name: Carvaka / Lokayata (position-entry)
dates_notes: This is a position-entry, not an author-entry. The Cārvāka / Lokāyata position is attested already in the late Vedic and early Buddhist period (the Pāyāsi-Suttanta of the Dīgha Nikāya engages a recognisably Lokāyata interlocutor, c. fifth century BCE) and continues to be cited as a live opponent into the late medieval period (Mādhava's Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha, fourteenth century). No primary Cārvāka treatise survives. The hypothetical Bārhaspatya-Sūtra is cited only fragmentarily in opponents. Jayarāśi's Tattvopaplava-Siṃha (eighth century) is sometimes classified as Cārvāka and sometimes as a sui generis sceptical treatise. The date-range given here covers the period of the position's attested presence as a recognised darśana.

lineage_polemical.note: Brahma-Sūtra 3.3.53–54 engages the Lokāyata-position that the self is identical with the body and refutes it; the engagement is brief but is the canonical Vedāntic acknowledgement of the position as a darśana to be answered.

Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 3.3.53 develops the anti-Lokāyata argument: Śaṅkara argues that consciousness cannot be reduced to a property of bodily elements because no material aggregate possesses cetanā in its constituent parts, and the supposed emergence of consciousness from acetana materials is unaccounted-for.

Madhva's Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya and Tattva-Viveka treat the Cārvāka position as the limit-case of nāstika error: the denial of post-mortem soul, of inference, and of śabda-pramāṇa together constitute the most thorough rejection of the Vedāntic frame.

core_thesis: *Cārvāka* (also *Lokāyata*, *Bārhaspatya*) names a *darśana*-position rather than an author. No primary text survives. The position is preserved through hostile citation in opponents — most notably Mādhava's *Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* ch. 1 (*Cārvāka-Darśana*, fourteenth century), the *Tattva-Saṅgraha* of Śāntarakṣita vv. 1857–1964 with Kamalaśīla's *Pañjikā* (eighth century, the *Lokāyata-parīkṣā*), Haribhadra's *Ṣaḍ-Darśana-Samuccaya* vv. 78–87 with Guṇaratna's *Tarka-Rahasya-Dīpikā* (fourteenth century), and Jayanta Bhaṭṭa's *Nyāya-Mañjarī* āhnika 1 (ninth century, on the *pramāṇa*-enumeration). Jayarāśi's *Tattvopaplava-Siṃha* (eighth century, the four *parīkṣā*-sections on *pratyakṣa*, *anumāna*, *upamāna*, *śabda*) is sometimes classified as Cārvāka but is more accurately a sui-generis sceptical treatise that uses Cārvāka-style arguments toward a destructive end. The reconstructible position holds four central theses. First, *pratyakṣa* (perception) is the sole *pramāṇa* (*Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* ch. 1, opening *pūrvapakṣa*); *anumāna* (inference) is rejected on the grounds that the *vyāpti*-relation between *hetu* and *sādhya* cannot itself be established without circularity (*Tattvopaplava-Siṃha*, *anumāna-parīkṣā*; *Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* ch. 1, *anumāna-khaṇḍana* section), and *śabda* and *upamāna* are rejected as derivative (*Tattvopaplava-Siṃha*, *śabda-parīkṣā* and *upamāna-parīkṣā*). Second, the *ātman* is identified with the conscious body (*Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* ch. 1, the *caitanya-viśiṣṭa-deha eva ātmā* fragment); consciousness arises from the conjunction of the four *mahā-bhūtas* (earth, water, fire, air) in the manner that intoxicating power arises from the fermentation of grains (*Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* ch. 1, the *kiṇva*-analogy fragment), and dissolves with bodily death — there is no post-mortem persistence of the soul (cf. *Brahma-Sūtra* 3.3.53–54, where the position is registered as the canonical *pūrvapakṣa* against the Vedāntic *ātman*). Third, *karma-phala-saṃbandha* and rebirth are rejected as inferential constructs without perceptual ground (*Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* ch. 1, the rejection of *paraloka* fragment). Fourth, *puruṣārtha* is reduced to *kāma* and *artha*, with *dharma* and *mokṣa* rejected as either Vedic-instrumental fictions or unattainable (*Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* ch. 1, the four-*puruṣārtha*-reduction). The popular characterisation as ethical hedonism is partly hostile-source-distortion: the surviving fragments stake an explicit rejection-of-inference epistemology and a naturalistic philosophy of mind, alongside the hedonistic-sounding *ṛṇaṃ kṛtvā ghṛtaṃ pibet* ("having taken on debt, drink ghee") fragment (preserved at *Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* ch. 1). The position should not be assimilated to modern "materialism" without qualification — the modern category imports baggage (mechanistic reduction, scientific naturalism) that is not present in the classical position; what is present is a categorial restriction of the *pramāṇas* and an ontological restriction of *ātman*.

engaged_works.title: Barhaspatya-Sutra (fragmentary, hostile-source-only)
alternate_titles: Lokāyata-Sūtra (alternative title used in some hostile sources)
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composition-date is essentially unconstrainable: the position is attested early (Pāyāsi-Suttanta) but the hypothetical Bārhaspatya-Sūtra survives only in fragments cited by later opponents.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: The Bārhaspatya-Sūtra is traditionally ascribed to Bṛhaspati but no continuous text survives; the fragments cited in opponents may or may not derive from a single sūtra-text, and some are likely later doxographer-reconstructions of the position rather than authentic sūtra-citations.

ascription_evidence.description: Bhattacharya (Studies on the Cārvāka / Lokāyata, 2011) catalogues the surviving fragments and discusses the methodological question of which are likely authentic sūtra-citations.

engaged_works.summary: No continuous text survives. The reconstructible position is preserved through hostile citation in (i) Mādhava's *Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* ch. 1 (the *Cārvāka-Darśana*, fourteenth century), the most extensive single source and the standard introduction to the position; (ii) the *Tattva-Saṅgraha* of Śāntarakṣita vv. 1857–1964 with Kamalaśīla's *Pañjikā* (eighth century, the *Lokāyata-parīkṣā*), which engages Cārvāka *pramāṇa*-doctrine in the chapter on inference; (iii) Haribhadra's *Ṣaḍ-Darśana-Samuccaya* vv. 78–87 with Guṇaratna's *Tarka-Rahasya-Dīpikā* (fourteenth century); (iv) Jayanta Bhaṭṭa's *Nyāya-Mañjarī* āhnika 1 (ninth century). Jayarāśi's *Tattvopaplava-Siṃha* (eighth century, structured as four sequential *parīkṣā*-sections — *pratyakṣa-parīkṣā*, *anumāna-parīkṣā*, *upamāna-parīkṣā*, *śabda-parīkṣā*) is a related but methodologically distinct sceptical treatise. The fragments preserved at *Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* ch. 1 include the perception-as-sole-pramāṇa thesis, the body-self identification (*caitanya-viśiṣṭa-deha eva ātmā*), the rejection of post-mortem soul and rebirth, the rejection of *karma-phala-saṃbandha*, the reduction of *puruṣārtha* to *kāma* and *artha*, and the famous *ṛṇaṃ kṛtvā ghṛtaṃ pibet* fragment. Modern reconstructive scholarship (Bhattacharya 2011, *Studies on the Cārvāka / Lokāyata*; Franco 1987, *Perception, Knowledge and Disbelief*) attempts to distinguish authentic *sūtra*-citations from later doxographer-additions. The position is engaged as a darśana — to be answered, not dismissed — by every later Indian doxographic and polemical work down to the early-modern period.

reception_notes: The Cārvāka position survives in tradition almost entirely as the *prima-facie* opponent (*pūrvapakṣa*) in opening chapters of polemical works — Mādhava's *Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha* opens with it; Haribhadra's *Ṣaḍ-Darśana-Samuccaya* engages it as the boundary-position; Vedāntic *bhāṣyas* engage it briefly at *Brahma-Sūtra* 3.3.53–54. The modern reception (Debiprasad Chattopadhyaya's *Lokāyata: A Study in Ancient Indian Materialism*, 1959) attempts to recover the position as Indian *materialism*; this reception is contested in current scholarship as importing modern materialist categories that distort the classical position.
